Owner_Name_If_Known: Eddie Sandoval (Sandoval Capital Inc.)
Distress_Evidence: Shut down 2020–Sep 2023 (3 years) for city code violations (electrical, planning, zoning). Owner filed lawsuit against City of El Paso during closure. Reopened Sep 30 2023 only after meeting compliance conditions. Preceded by 2019 fire. 3-year forced closure + costly legal battle + ongoing regulatory burden = financially/emotionally fatigued owner. (Source: KFOX14; CBS4 Local)
Real_Estate_Upside: 9 acres on US-62/180 (Alameda Ave) — primary east-west highway connecting to I-10. El Paso's Lower Valley is one of the fastest-growing US-Mexico border corridors. Nearshoring/border trade driving industrial and logistics development on this corridor.
Hispanic_Population_Context: ZIP 79907 (Lower Valley) is 95%+ Hispanic; Bronco Swap Meet operating since 1985 with 300+ vendors and 500+ dependent families — deeply embedded in the Hispanic economy
Investment_Thesis: Battle-worn owner of a 9-acre highway site who just survived 3 years of city enforcement hell — Sandoval is a motivated seller waiting for the right conversation.
Risks_Red_Flags: El Paso is well outside Tulsa metro; regulatory history requires due diligence on current compliance status; verify permit standing before approach
Notes: Direct outreach to Eddie Sandoval / Sandoval Capital Inc. — reference the reopening hardship to open the conversation

Notes: Contact broker via LoopNet listing ID 16700123. Highest-priority Austin lead.

Market_Name: Expo New Mexico Open Air Flea Market
Asking_Price: Not listed (state-operated; concession contract is the acquisition target)
Owner_Name_If_Known: State of New Mexico (Expo NM); flea market operated under concession arrangement
Distress_Evidence: March 23, 2026: State Fairgrounds District Board approved first phase of redevelopment of the 236-acre Expo NM site — plans include housing, park, and sports stadium. State Fair expected to relocate. Weekly flea market faces displacement. Vendors and community advocates publicly warned at board meetings. Redevelopment is 'years off' per former Mayor Chavez but concession contract is now under existential uncertainty. (Source: NM Governor press release March 2026)
Real_Estate_Upside: Opportunity to acquire operator goodwill, vendor relationships, and customer base at distressed concession values and reposition at new highway site (Westside Albuquerque, I-25/I-40 interchange) before displacement creates a vendor vacuum. Arizona Market Place model at a new Albuquerque location.
Hispanic_Population_Context: Albuquerque is 47-49% Hispanic (~268,000 people). San Pedro NE neighborhood skews higher. Market serves primarily Hispanic working-class vendors and shoppers.
Investment_Thesis: NM's largest outdoor flea market being displaced by a state-approved redevelopment plan — window to acquire operator goodwill and vendor base at distressed values and reposition at a highway site before displacement creates a vendor vacuum.
Risks_Red_Flags: Not a traditional private sale; requires navigating state concession contract; timeline is multi-year; operator identity not publicly confirmed; community opposition to relocation is organized; no asking price or valuation available
Source: NM Governor Press Release March 2026 / Source NM / NM Political Report
Notes: Contact Expo NM at (505) 222-9700 to identify the flea market concession operator. This is an advance-positioning play, not an immediate acquisition.

Market_Name: Yuma Swap Meet (Displaced — Seeking New Site)
Asking_Price: Not listed (former site is federal; displaced operator/vendors are the acquisition target)
Owner_Name_If_Known: Not publicly confirmed; sold to US Dept of Homeland Security / CBP Oct 2023
Contact_Info: Phone: (928) 782-1331 (per Yelp) | No active website confirmed
Distress_Evidence: October 2023: forced permanent closure when owners sold property to US federal government (DHS/CBP) for border patrol facility expansion. Closure was abrupt — vendors denied final access to retrieve inventory. Operator stated 'in search of a new location' Oct 2023. As of March 2026, no confirmed reopening. Only 60-70 of 350 vendors found temporary home at Arizona Market Place. 280+ vendors still unplaced 2+ years later. (Source: AZFamily Oct/Nov 2023; KYMA Oct 2023)
Real_Estate_Upside: First investor to secure highway-adjacent land in Yuma (S 4th Ave corridor or I-8/US-95 interchange) and pitch the 280+ displaced vendors has first-mover monopoly on a proven market with existing vendor pipeline. Arizona Market Place is seasonal (Nov-Apr only) — year-round outdoor market has no real Yuma competition for 7+ months per year.
Hispanic_Population_Context: Yuma County is approximately 60% Hispanic. Market served binational Yuma/San Luis community. Named vendors Maria Elena Machado (15 years) and Maria Chavez reflect the predominantly Hispanic vendor base.
Investment_Thesis: A 60-year-old swap meet forced out by the federal government left 350 displaced Hispanic vendors and an unserved customer base in a 60%-Hispanic border city — first investor to secure a Yuma highway parcel and approach the original operators captures a ready-built market with no year-round competition.
Risks_Red_Flags: Original operator identity not publicly confirmed; 280 unplaced vendors may have dispersed over 2+ years; new site needs zoning approval; AZ summer heat limits outdoor viability May-October
Notes: Call (928) 782-1331 to identify original operator. Approach unplaced vendors through Arizona Market Place as a contact pipeline.

Owner_Name_If_Known: Jim and Debbie Fitzgerald (26+ years)
Distress_Evidence: Devastating fire July 16 2022 destroyed most of the 26,000 sqft lot (Hidalgo County Fire Marshal: cause undetermined). Vendors lost $80k-$100k inventory each. One vendor: 'This was our financial retirement.' Multiple vendors permanently departed. Market rebuilding in reduced capacity since late 2022 after 26+ years of operation. (Source: KRGV)
Real_Estate_Upside: Directly on Business Hwy 83 — primary RGV commercial corridor connecting Brownsville through McAllen. Hidalgo County gained 100k+ residents 2010–2020, one of the fastest-growing US counties. Post-fire minimal footprint = ideal ground-up redevelopment or rezoning.
Hispanic_Population_Context: Donna is 92.5% Hispanic. Hidalgo County is 91.9% Hispanic — one of the most densely Hispanic markets in the US.
Investment_Thesis: Fire-damaged, 26-year family market on RGV's prime highway with owners who called it their 'financial retirement' — motivated seller on a Business Hwy 83 parcel in the fastest-growing Hispanic county in Texas.
Risks_Red_Flags: Owners may be reinvesting in recovery rather than exiting; no public listing; Donna commercial land values lower than major metros; direct outreach required
Notes: Email fitzgeraldjim7@aol.com directly. Reference fire recovery and discuss long-term plans.

Owner_Name_If_Known: Vickie L. Roberts (operator); founded by Peggy and Dwight Hood Roberts (retired)
Distress_Evidence: November 2024 windstorm caused major structural damage — multiple buildings destroyed, metal roof and walls blown off. Market was operating as legal non-conforming use since 2006 annexation (zoned Agricultural) — could not legally rebuild without rezoning. Belton City Council unanimously approved rezoning to Commercial-1 with SUP in early 2025. Founding generation (Peggy & Dwight) already retired; now 35+ year family operation run by next gen facing capital rebuild decision at moment of peak land value. (Source: KXXV; KDH News)
Real_Estate_Upside: 9.71 acres of freshly rezoned Commercial-1 land directly off I-35 at the I-35/I-14 junction. TxDOT widening I-35 Temple to Belton from 6 to 14 lanes. Belton EDC actively funding commercial infrastructure on I-35 and I-14 corridor. (Source: TxDOT; Belton EDC)
Hispanic_Population_Context: Belton is 32.6% Hispanic; Bell County ~29% Hispanic. Killeen-Temple-Fort Hood metro has a significant and growing Hispanic population.
Investment_Thesis: 9.71 acres of freshly rezoned Commercial-1 land on an I-35 corridor being widened to 14 lanes — storm-damaged family operation at peak land value creates a textbook pre-sale capital decision.
Risks_Red_Flags: Hispanic % at 32.6% is lower than other leads; owners received rebuild approval and may proceed; no for-sale listing confirmed; Hispanic market less concentrated than RGV/SA leads
Notes: Call Vickie Roberts at (254) 939-6411. Reference storm damage and rezoning — ask about rebuild timeline vs. sale interest.
